purpose: Decomposes dollar dominance into four distinct dimensions to avoid conflating marginal reserve reallocation with structural displacement.
components: Reserve allocation: declining 0.5-0.8pp/year — factual but tactically irrelevant against $7.5T daily FX turnover
Trade invoicing: stable — USD still dominant in commodity and manufactured goods invoicing
Financial intermediation: stable to growing — USD involved in 88% of FX turnover, unchanged over 20 years
Settlement infrastructure: challenged — CIPS growth real but inflated by domestic volumes, lacks CNY safe-asset depth
current_assessment: Reserve allocation is declining at the margin but all other dimensions remain stable or growing. CNY reserve share plateaued at 2.3-2.7%. Gold purchases reflect portfolio rebalancing and sanctions-proofing, not strategic dollar replacement. Sterling-to-dollar transition analogue took 30+ years requiring conditions China does not meet.
limitations: Reserve allocation decline could accelerate non-linearly if geopolitical conditions change
Difficult to weight the four dimensions — does settlement infrastructure matter more than reserves?
CIPS volume data quality is questionable (domestic vs. international volume mix)
Framework is descriptive; does not predict transition triggers or thresholds
